questions.question: You are on a cross-country flight at 11,000 ft. You feel slightly euphoric and your peripheral vision seems to be narrowing. Your passenger says you look 'spaced out'. What is the most likely cause?
options: Onset of hypoxia due to decreased partial pressure of oxygen at this altitude
Hyperventilation due to the stress of navigation
Fatigue accumulated since the start of the flight
A side effect of the coffee you drank before departure
questions.correct_answer: Onset of hypoxia due to decreased partial pressure of oxygen at this altitude
questions.explanation: Early hypoxia symptoms appear from 10,000 ft: euphoria, tunnel vision, reduced coordination. The pilot is unaware of their own degradation — which is why the passenger's observation is valuable. Hyperventilation is caused by stress, not altitude.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Hyperventilation is linked to stress, not altitude — and its symptoms include tingling and spasms
distractors_rationale.option_c: Fatigue does not cause tunnel vision or euphoria
distractors_rationale.option_d: Caffeine does not have these specific effects

questions.question: You climb to 25,000 ft in an unpressurized aircraft. Your emergency oxygen fails. How much time do you have to perform an emergency descent before losing consciousness?
options: 2 to 5 minutes
20 to 30 minutes
30 to 60 seconds
10 to 15 minutes
questions.explanation: At 25,000 ft, Time of Useful Consciousness (TUC) is 2 to 5 minutes. At 18,000 ft it is 20-30 min, at 30,000 ft it drops to 30-60 seconds. These values must be memorized by every PPL pilot.

distractors_rationale.option_b: 20-30 minutes is the TUC at 18,000 ft, not 25,000 ft
distractors_rationale.option_c: 30-60 seconds is the TUC at 30,000 ft
distractors_rationale.option_d: 10-15 minutes does not correspond to any standard TUC

questions.question: In flight, you are suddenly faced with an emergency situation. You feel dizzy, tingling in your hands, and blurred vision. You are at 3,000 ft. What is the most likely cause?
options: Hyperventilation due to the stress of the emergency
Hypoxia due to altitude
Onset of hypoglycemia
Carbon monoxide poisoning
questions.correct_answer: Hyperventilation due to the stress of the emergency
questions.explanation: At 3,000 ft, hypoxia is impossible. The symptoms (dizziness, tingling, blurred vision) in a stressful situation are typical of hyperventilation. Rapid breathing lowers blood CO2 levels. The solution: slow your breathing, speak out loud, breathe into cupped hands.

distractors_rationale.option_b: At 3,000 ft, oxygen partial pressure is still sufficient — hypoxia only appears from 10,000 ft
distractors_rationale.option_c: Hypoglycemia causes fatigue and tremors, not tingling
distractors_rationale.option_d: CO poisoning causes headaches and nausea, not tingling

questions.question: During descent to an airfield, you feel increasing pain in your right ear. What is the first action to take?
options: Perform the Valsalva maneuver (pinch nose and blow gently) and reduce the rate of descent
Continue the descent normally, the pain will go away on its own at landing
Climb back immediately and declare an emergency to ATC
Increase the rate of descent to get through the painful zone faster
questions.correct_answer: Perform the Valsalva maneuver (pinch nose and blow gently) and reduce the rate of descent
questions.explanation: The Valsalva maneuver equalizes pressure between the middle ear and the outside. If pain persists, reduce the rate of descent or climb slightly. Never force a descent with ear pain — barotrauma can become incapacitating.

distractors_rationale.option_b: The pain will not go away automatically — it can worsen up to eardrum rupture
distractors_rationale.option_c: Climbing back immediately is excessive — first try Valsalva and reduce descent rate
distractors_rationale.option_d: Increasing the descent rate worsens the pressure differential and the pain

questions.question: You have a mild cold but decide to fly anyway. During descent, you feel a violent pain in your forehead. What happened?
options: Sinus congestion prevented pressure equalization, causing sinus barotrauma
The cold turned into acute sinusitis due to the pressure change
It is a normal headache related to flight fatigue
The cold air at altitude froze your sinuses
questions.correct_answer: Sinus congestion prevented pressure equalization, causing sinus barotrauma
questions.explanation: A cold congests sinus mucous membranes, blocking equalization channels. During descent, external pressure increases but cannot equalize in congested sinuses, causing violent pain. A pilot with a cold should postpone their flight.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Sinusitis is an infection that takes time to develop, not a few minutes
distractors_rationale.option_c: A normal headache does not cause 'violent' pain localized to the forehead
distractors_rationale.option_d: Sinuses do not freeze — cabin temperature remains above freezing

questions.question: You are preparing for a night flight. You are in the brightly lit briefing room, then you go out on the tarmac to turn off the parking lights. You can barely see anything. How long must you wait for your night vision to be optimal?
options: 30 minutes — the time for rods to adapt to darkness
2 minutes — the time for your pupils to dilate
10 minutes — the time for cones to adapt
Immediately — night vision is instant once you step out of the light
questions.correct_answer: 30 minutes — the time for rods to adapt to darkness
questions.explanation: Rods (night vision) take 30 minutes to fully adapt to darkness. White light destroys this adaptation instantly. This is why you should use a red light in the cockpit at night and avoid looking at white lights.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Pupil dilation is fast but not enough — the chemical adaptation of rods takes 30 min
distractors_rationale.option_c: Cones (daylight vision) adapt quickly but are not responsible for night vision
distractors_rationale.option_d: Night vision is not instantaneous — it requires progressive adaptation

questions.concept_title: Vestibular Illusion and False Attitude Sensation
questions.question: On a night flight with no outside visibility, you perform a slow coordinated turn. After a few seconds, you feel like the aircraft is level when it is still turning. Why?
options: The inner ear detects accelerations but not constant speeds — the slow turn is no longer perceived once established
The eyes get used to the movement and no longer detect the turn
The brain automatically compensates for the turn to maintain balance
The attitude indicator has reset itself
questions.correct_answer: The inner ear detects accelerations but not constant speeds — the slow turn is no longer perceived once established
questions.explanation: The semicircular canals of the inner ear detect angular accelerations, not constant speeds. Once the turn is established at a constant rate, the inner ear 'forgets' the turn. If you abruptly recover, you will have the false sensation of climbing when level. The only reliable reference is the instrument panel.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Eyes do not get used to movement to the point of not detecting it
distractors_rationale.option_c: The brain does not 'compensate' for turns — it interprets signals from the inner ear
distractors_rationale.option_d: The attitude indicator does not reset itself

questions.question: While crossing a cloud layer, you suddenly lose all outside visual reference. You have a strong sensation that the aircraft is descending while the attitude indicator shows level flight. What is the golden rule?
options: Trust the instruments, never your sensations — maintain the indicated attitude
Pull gently on the yoke to counter the sensation of descent
Push slightly on the yoke to follow the perceived descent
Make a 180° turn to get out of the clouds as quickly as possible
questions.correct_answer: Trust the instruments, never your sensations — maintain the indicated attitude
questions.explanation: Spatial disorientation is the leading cause of fatal accidents in general aviation. The golden rule is absolute: when your sensations contradict the instruments, trust the instruments. Do not attempt abrupt maneuvers. Contact ATC for radar assistance to reach VMC conditions.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Pulling on the yoke based on a sensation is exactly what causes loss of control
distractors_rationale.option_c: Pushing on the yoke worsens the situation if the sensation is false
distractors_rationale.option_d: A 180° turn without visual reference is dangerous without IMC training

questions.question: You are on your 4th hour of flying on a cross-country day. You start making heading calculation errors, you forget to change frequencies, and you jump when ATC calls you. What is happening?
options: You are in an advanced state of fatigue with risk of micro-sleeps — it is time to land and take a break
This is normal after 4 hours of flying, keep going, it will pass
You need caffeine — drink strong coffee to regain alertness
You have a vision problem related to eye strain
questions.correct_answer: You are in an advanced state of fatigue with risk of micro-sleeps — it is time to land and take a break
questions.explanation: Fatigue reduces cognitive performance: judgment errors, increased reaction time, micro-sleeps. The pilot is a poor judge of their own fatigue level. Repeated errors and startles are alarm signals. The only solution is to land and rest.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Fatigue does not 'pass' by continuing to fly — it worsens
distractors_rationale.option_c: Coffee masks symptoms but does not eliminate fatigue — and caffeine worsens dehydration
distractors_rationale.option_d: The described symptoms are cognitive (errors, forgetfulness, startles), not visual

questions.question: This morning you woke up with a mild sore throat. You took a tablet to treat it. You are stressed about your practical exam this afternoon. You slept well for 8 hours and had a good breakfast. According to the I'M SAFE checklist, can you fly?
options: No — Illness (sore throat) and Medication (tablet) are two positive items, the flight must be postponed
Yes — you slept well and ate well, the two most important items are OK
Yes — a mild sore throat is not a contraindication to flying
No — exam stress is disqualifying
questions.correct_answer: No — Illness (sore throat) and Medication (tablet) are two positive items, the flight must be postponed
questions.explanation: The I'M SAFE checklist says: if any single item is positive (a problem), the flight must be postponed. Here, Illness (sore throat) AND Medication (unknown effects) are two alarm signals. Exam stress is normal and not disqualifying alone, but combined with the other two items, the decision is clear: do not fly.

distractors_rationale.option_b: All items are important — a single 'yes' justifies postponement
distractors_rationale.option_c: A sore throat can worsen in flight (sinus barotrauma) and medication may have side effects
distractors_rationale.option_d: Moderate stress is not disqualifying alone — but combined with Illness and Medication, it is too much

questions.question: You attended a party last night and had your last drink at 11:00 PM. You need to take command at 10:00 AM the next morning. Are you compliant with EASA regulations?
options: Yes, 11 hours have passed — but it is strongly recommended to wait 24h after a heavy drinking session
No, 12 hours minimum are required — you are not compliant
Yes, 11 hours is enough if you no longer feel the alcohol
No, 24 hours are required in all cases
questions.correct_answer: No, 12 hours minimum are required — you are not compliant
questions.explanation: The EASA rule is a minimum of 12 hours (bottle-to-throttle) between the last drink and taking command. From 11:00 PM to 10:00 AM, 11 hours have passed — you are not compliant. Additionally, after a heavy drinking session, it is recommended to wait 24h due to residual effects on judgment.

distractors_rationale.option_a: 11h is insufficient — the minimum is 12h, not 11h
distractors_rationale.option_c: Not 'feeling' the alcohol does not mean blood alcohol is below 0.2 g/L
distractors_rationale.option_d: 24h is a recommendation for heavy drinking sessions, not a regulatory requirement

questions.question: You are on a cross-country flight on a hot summer day. You brought a thermos of coffee and a bottle of water. Mid-flight, you start getting a headache and cramps in your calves. What is the most likely cause?
options: Onset of dehydration — you should drink water regularly, not just coffee
Normal fatigue after 2 hours of flying
Lack of sugar — you should eat a granola bar
Heat stroke due to outside temperature
questions.correct_answer: Onset of dehydration — you should drink water regularly, not just coffee
questions.explanation: Dry air at altitude and cabin ventilation accelerate dehydration. Coffee worsens the problem due to caffeine (diuretic). The symptoms (headache, cramps) are typical. Drink water in small regular sips, do not wait until thirsty.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Cramps and headaches are not symptoms of normal fatigue
distractors_rationale.option_c: Hypoglycemia causes tremors and irritability, not cramps
distractors_rationale.option_d: Heat stroke causes fever and nausea, not specifically cramps

questions.question: You skipped breakfast to arrive on time for your morning flight. After 1h30 of flying, you start trembling, you are irritable, and you have trouble concentrating on your navigation. What should you do?
options: Eat a granola bar or fruit that you brought in your flight bag
Drink a sugary soda to raise your blood sugar quickly
Continue flying, these symptoms will pass with habit
Land immediately at the nearest airfield
questions.correct_answer: Eat a granola bar or fruit that you brought in your flight bag
questions.explanation: The symptoms (trembling, irritability, concentration difficulty) are typical of hypoglycemia. The solution is simple: eat a snack. This is why it is recommended to carry granola bars or fruit in the cabin. A sugary soda would raise blood sugar too quickly then cause a crash.

distractors_rationale.option_b: A sugary soda causes a spike then a sharp drop in blood sugar — not ideal in flight
distractors_rationale.option_c: The symptoms will not pass — they will worsen over time
distractors_rationale.option_d: Emergency landing is excessive — eating a snack is sufficient in most cases

questions.question: You have seasonal allergies and your pharmacist recommends an over-the-counter antihistamine. The label says 'may cause drowsiness'. What do you do?
options: You do not take this medication before flying and consult your AME for a flight-compatible treatment
You take it anyway — it is over-the-counter, so it is safe
You take it but reduce the dose by half to limit drowsiness
You take it the evening before the flight so the effect wears off by morning
questions.correct_answer: You do not take this medication before flying and consult your AME for a flight-compatible treatment
questions.explanation: An over-the-counter medication is not necessarily compatible with flying. The warning 'may cause drowsiness' is an alarm signal. Even a medication prescribed by a general practitioner may be prohibited in flight. The only reference is the AME (AeroMedical Examiner).

distractors_rationale.option_b: Over-the-counter does not mean compatible with flying — many OTC drugs are prohibited
distractors_rationale.option_c: Reducing the dose does not guarantee the absence of side effects — and the dosage is not designed to be modified
distractors_rationale.option_d: Residual effects can persist the next day — and some antihistamines have a long half-life

questions.question: You are in flight and an emergency occurs. You feel your heart racing and your attention focusing solely on the problem, to the point of forgetting to fly the aircraft. What is happening and what should you do?
options: You are in excessive stress with attention tunneling — use controlled breathing and prioritize: aviate first, communicate next, procedure after
This is a normal reaction — keep focusing on the problem, it is the priority
You are having a panic attack — declare an emergency and land immediately
You are hyperventilating — breathe into a paper bag
questions.correct_answer: You are in excessive stress with attention tunneling — use controlled breathing and prioritize: aviate first, communicate next, procedure after
questions.explanation: Excessive stress degrades performance: attention tunneling, forgotten procedures. The 'Aviate, Navigate, Communicate' prioritization is the answer. Controlled breathing (inhale 4s, exhale 6s) helps reduce stress. Do not let stress make you forget to fly.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Tunneling is dangerous — it makes you forget to fly the aircraft
distractors_rationale.option_c: Declaring an emergency and landing may be necessary, but first regain control of the aircraft
distractors_rationale.option_d: Hyperventilation is a possible consequence, but the main problem is excessive stress

questions.question: You are preparing for a 2-hour flight at 8,000 ft. You had a large breakfast with carbonated drinks. During the climb, you feel abdominal pain and gas. What is the cause?
options: Gas expansion in your stomach and intestines due to decreased pressure at altitude (Boyle's Law)
Food poisoning from breakfast
Onset of abdominal hypoxia
Stress from takeoff disrupting your digestion
questions.correct_answer: Gas expansion in your stomach and intestines due to decreased pressure at altitude (Boyle's Law)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law states that gas volume is inversely proportional to pressure. As you climb, pressure decreases and gases expand. A heavy meal and carbonated drinks before flight worsen the phenomenon. Solution: light meal and no carbonated drinks before flying.

distractors_rationale.option_b: Food poisoning does not occur so quickly after a meal
distractors_rationale.option_c: Abdominal hypoxia does not exist — hypoxia is a lack of oxygen in tissues
distractors_rationale.option_d: Stress can disrupt digestion but does not cause such specific pain during climb

questions.question: You are on a 3-hour flight in summer at 7,000 ft. On the ground it was 30°C, you left in shorts and a t-shirt. After 2 hours, you have trouble handling the controls precisely and you are shivering. What happened?
options: The temperature dropped about 14°C from the ground (2°C per 1,000 ft) — you are in early hypothermia
This is normal in summer at this altitude — keep going, you will get used to it
You caught a cold from the wind — turn the heater on full
You are dehydrated — drink hot water
questions.correct_answer: The temperature dropped about 14°C from the ground (2°C per 1,000 ft) — you are in early hypothermia
questions.explanation: Temperature decreases by approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft. At 7,000 ft, it is about 14°C cooler than on the ground. With 30°C on the ground, it is about 16°C at altitude. Wind chill makes it feel even colder. Hypothermia reduces dexterity and judgment. Always carry warm clothing, even in summer.

distractors_rationale.option_b: No, the symptoms will not pass — hypothermia worsens over time
distractors_rationale.option_c: Cabin heat can help, but the main problem is lack of appropriate clothing
distractors_rationale.option_d: Drinking hot water does not solve the problem — you need warm clothing
